column2: GitHub archive data/aleabitoreddit_tweets.json — preserves canonical X URLs + ISO timestamps
chunked chronologically into 16 windows; individual thread trees not rebuilt (parent_post_id mostly unavailable in archive)
1 row(s) unverifiable post_id. Direct X.com fetch BLOCKED (HTTP 402) — IDs verified against archive, not re-fetched from X.
All publicly accessible posts retrieved during this research run via the third-party archive; NOT all posts ever published. Deleted/private/rate-limited/pre-2025-07 posts not captured.

column12: evidence_excerpt
column13: summary

column12: $UPWK is trading at a 7.5 P/E with ~$770M in annual revenue, 78% gross margins, and $622M in cash — yet its market cap is only $1.74B. Who cares about revenue growth when they can just scale operating margins?
column13: He frames Upwork as undervalued on cash/margins, arguing operating-margin expansion matters more than top-line growth.
column16: explicit. Business-quality vs price; margin lever as value thesis.

column12: If Upwork can scale operating margins from 20% → 40–60%.. That’s $770M x 60% = $462M operating income. At a modest 15x EBIT multiple, that's a $6.9B valuation. VS today's $1.7B market cap, that’s a potential 3–4x upside.
column13: He builds an explicit EBIT-multiple valuation bridge to size upside versus current market cap.
column16: explicit. Translates margin assumptions into target valuation.

column12: $ALAB is...the only small cap company...with systemic exposure to 5 of the Mag7...growing ~150%+ y/y, with 76% margins...NVDA started at a 150B market cap -> 4 TRILLION+ because hyperscalers needed GPUs. We're the start of this effect with ALAB for the AI supply chain
column13: He picks a small supplier with outsized exposure to giant hyperscaler customers, analogizing to early NVDA.
column16: explicit. Asymmetric small-cap supplier to giant customers.

column12: Changed my mind on $ALAB, took profit at $217 so exited long and opened up short with $CREDO as a hedge. ALAB feels a bit overbought after going up 14%.
column13: After a rally he exits ALAB and flips to a hedged short, judging it overbought.
column16: explicit. Re-evaluation after price appreciation; thesis revision.

column12: When I was doing arbitrage earlier, it was clear $BULL was going to crash from $70 once warrants could be redeemed...$10 strikes ($4.00 each) were so mispriced relative to the actual stock. TLDR: dilution crashes stocks!
column13: He explains a warrant-vs-stock arbitrage and how lockup/dilution mechanics drive price collapse.
column16: explicit. Float/dilution mechanics as core process.

column12: People keep DMing me about new IPOs. Advice: PAY ATTENTION to dilution if you're short/long...$CRCL has a 180 day lockup as well, people are trading with limited floats, which is why it has a 40B MC.
column13: He stresses tracking IPO lockups and limited floats as the driver of distorted market caps.
column16: explicit. Process rule on IPO float distortion.

column12: As for thesis on Blacksky ($BKSY) honestly, pretty terrible company. However, Planet Labs $PL + Satellite/Space companies like $RKLB $RDW had a huge spike...Blacksky (industry adjacent) hadn't moved at all yet. It ended up catching up in price
column13: He buys a weak company purely on a sector catch-up / rotation thesis, not fundamentals.

column12: $ETORO is a great add at $63.1, crypto brokerages like $HOOD and $BULL are both up 8-10%+ while ETORO is down 1.25%. Usually related sectors have similar movements, it's more of playing catch-up
column13: He trades sector co-movement, buying the laggard within a correlated peer group.

column12: Market makers flushed Upwork call open interest for August 15th, and now it's rallying where it should be above $17.5+. A 1.8B software company with ~80% gross margins doing 770m in annual revenue is almost free money.
column13: He attributes price suppression to option-flow/OI flush and frames the post-expiry rally as a mispricing catalyst.
column16: explicit. Catalyst + market-awareness gap via option mechanics.

column12: $SG dropping 8.2% looks option flow-driven (10k+ $8 puts 9/19 caused MM shorts), not fundamental changes. Hedging likely overshot the selloff, so I'd call it a buyable dip since drop was due to short term hedging.
column13: He distinguishes an option-hedging-driven selloff from a fundamental one, treating the dealer-hedge overshoot as buyable.
column16: explicit. Separates mechanical flow from fundamentals.

column12: If you're scared about $NBIS remember DILUTION WAS ALREADY PRICED IN. The $MSFT contract was 17B+, increasing forward rev by 300%+ for over 7 years. $NBIS gained 45% on the day not 250%+.
column13: He argues the dilution is already priced and the contract-driven forward revenue is underappreciated relative to the modest pop.
column16: explicit. Market-awareness gap reasoning on dilution.

column12: Short interest ⏫ to 34.59% of float...HIMS acquired 1.3M+ customers across Europe through the Zava acqusition, which forecasts haven't priced in yet...TTM Revenue ~$2.01 billion (up ~88.7%). Growth will ultimately screw the short position.
column13: He uses measurable short interest, acquisition customer counts, and revenue growth to argue shorts must cover.
column16: explicit. Measurable evidence (SI, rev, customers) underpins thesis.

column12: 36%+ short interest means hedge funds are nearing the limits of how much they can borrow to short...it’s simply a race to see which hedge funds cover their shorts first - and that’s when the short squeeze begins.
column13: He reasons borrow availability and short-interest limits set the timing for a squeeze.
column16: explicit. Borrow constraints as measurable chokepoint.

column12: If you're debating $ORCL vs $AMZN|$GOOGL, $AMD|$AVGO vs $NVDA, $NBIS vs $IREN...It's easy. Just buy $TSM, everything above is dependent on it. Undervalued and a monopoly
column13: He works the supply chain back to its common dependency, TSM, framing it as the foundational chokepoint everything runs on.
column16: explicit. Reverse-maps the chain to the indispensable foundry.

column12: and... $AMD runs on $TSM.
column13: He completes the chain reasoning, noting even AMD ultimately depends on TSM.
column16: explicit. Tail of the reverse-map logic to foundry.

column12: $TSM will probably rally to $1.8T market cap...$1.3T -> $1.8T given all the backlogs from Oracle/NBIS, etc. after reading all earnings. I almost never buy calls aside from $GOOG...But IV is decent ~32
column13: He cites order backlogs from downstream buyers (read across earnings calls) as evidence for the foundry chokepoint thesis.

column12: For $HIMS it’s mainly because short sellers shorted another 2.5%+ of the float to prevent the stock from breaking out from $50 and triggering a parabolic squeeze. ~42% of the stock is now sold short and they’re trapped if buying pressure continued.
column13: He reads incremental short-selling as deliberate breakout suppression near a key level.

column12: Read $RDDT, X, WSJ...to get info on upcoming events like earnings. But IGNORE the sentiment since it's usually wrong...Know what a real catalyst is and buy beforehand. Real = 2B+ from S&P flowing into $HOOD MC on inclusion. Fake = CFO resigning
column13: He codifies his process: source upcoming events, ignore sentiment, and distinguish real (flow-moving) catalysts from fake ones, buying beforehand.
column16: explicit. Core catalyst-vs-noise framework; selection of catalysts.

column12: I don't go out of my way to search up Litecoin ETFs...just browsing online people...were talking about it getting rejected. -> Search up Litecoin ETF filing, find it's Oct 2nd -> Search up Polymarket + Bloomberg Odds...ignore it. Made my own decision that it's consequential enough of a catalyst
column13: He describes how he sources a catalyst idea, verifies dates and Polymarket/Bloomberg odds, and overrides crowd sentiment.
column16: explicit. How he chooses and verifies a research target.

column12: When 1.4m+ Circle shares had early unlock, the price crashed back down to $130...The full 180 day lock up is December 2nd...$10B+ USD of potential selling pressure in two months...Lesson: Float & fundamentals > lines on a chart.
column13: He uses float, lockup schedules, and a peer revenue-share comparison (CRCL vs COIN) to argue valuation and frame a pair trade.
column16: explicit. Float/fundamentals over TA; comparable-value pair logic.

column12: NVDA sees other hyperscalers like AMZN, GOOGL building out their own chips...Answer? Build out GPU lenders eg CRWV...Access to GPUs becomes increasingly through these lenders...NVIDIA has every reason to inflate GPU lenders like CoreWeave and NBIS to hedge against hyperscaler custom chips.
column13: He maps where value accrues in the AI-compute chain, arguing NVDA structurally backs neocloud GPU-lenders to defend its moat, ranking NBIS over peers.
column16: explicit. Compares value capture across compute layers; incentive-driven moat. Major thesis post.

column12: $NBIS and $CRWV are NVIDIA's solution to protecting it's moat and GPU margin compression...supporting companies like NBIS on purpose to diversify demand from hyperscalers and prevent margin compression from Azure, AWS by spreading out dependencies
column13: He explains the incentive structure where NVDA props up neoclouds to prevent hyperscaler concentration and margin compression.
column16: explicit. Layer dynamics and pricing-power reasoning.

column12: It's not because of a price rally...$NBIS has 17B from $MSFT for multiple years, $4.1B+ now raised for capex...before $50 NBIS was speculation and I wouldn't have high conviction...Now it's pure scaling from here
column13: He re-underwrites NBIS post-rally, arguing a financing/contract de-risking event (not price) justifies higher conviction at a higher price.
column16: explicit. Why he buys higher after a catalyst removes risk.

column12: $3B deal was over 10y, compared to $NBIS $17B for 5 years. 1B+ dilution news...0% interest (unlike $CRWV 9%) is really positive. It's at 38%+ above current market rate at $16.06. This is how you do dilution. Very low debt on balance sheet too
column13: He compares contract duration, dilution terms, interest rates, and balance-sheet debt across CIFR/NBIS/CRWV to judge a buyable dip.
column16: explicit. Above-market financing as bullish tell; comparative terms.

column12: $NUAI...sold 12.9M shares for ~$8.6M...but have a EPFA for $1B in shares to 'ATW AI LLC' for 95% under market rate...763 million shares pending dilution...This is probably one of the worst toxic retail shareholder dilutive financing I've seen
column13: He reads the actual financing structure (EPFA, 95%-discount issuance, share authorization) to reject the stock as toxic dilution despite upside.
column16: explicit. Filing-level dilution analysis kills the thesis.

column12: NVDA/TSM (2022->): GPU for hyperscalers; CRDO/ALAB (2024->): hyperscaler wins -> parabolic growth; NBIS/CIFR/IREN (2025-): AWS/Azure -> parabolic growth from AI compute...ABOVE current price funding is a bullish tell.
column13: He lays out the full neocloud thesis: a capex funnel from hyperscalers to small suppliers, sequenced over time, with above-price financing as a confirming signal and an explicit bear-case checklist.
column16: explicit. Master thesis: demand funnel, layer sequencing, risk enumeration.

column12: AI consumption is a great catalyst for energy companies too for like $VST or $CEG...$NFE...Main issue was debt but projected 3 rate cuts will help reduce their floating interest.
column13: He extends from AI compute to the next limiting layer, power/energy, and weighs floating-rate debt against rate-cut tailwinds.
column16: explicit. Moves to power as the next AI bottleneck layer.

column12: Then it might end up like $SMCI in 2-3 years...high revenue, lower margins, but inventory risk and profit growth once all the big tech deploys their own chips with $TSM + $AVGO.
column13: He maps substitute risk: hyperscaler custom chips eventually compress neocloud margins and create inventory risk over a 2-3 year horizon.
column16: explicit. Vertical-integration substitution risk over time.

column12: These are contracts already locked in like 5Y for 17 Billion but markets are always forward looking so after 4 years if it looks like they'll start to pivot off like how apple did with Qualcomm, we'll see a revaluation.
column13: He uses the Apple-Qualcomm precedent to time when substitution risk (in-housing) would trigger a forward-looking re-rating.
column16: explicit. Contract-expiry as substitution-risk timing marker.

column12: with $RKLB he doesn't quite get the return potential from medium lift payloads + service potential from Starlink/flatlites, which isn't baked into typical forward rev
column13: He claims an edge from understanding RKLB's medium-lift payload and services economics that standard forward-revenue models miss.
column16: explicit. Translates technical product roadmap into under-modeled revenue.

column12: I don't really have a wide portfolio, I focus on a few high convictions stocks with great fundamentals + potential and shift around.
column13: He describes a concentrated, conviction-weighted portfolio approach rather than broad diversification.

column12: I don’t touch biotech since I’m not educated on it.
column13: He restricts himself to domains where he has expertise, declining biotech.
column16: explicit. How he chooses research domains.

column12: I run a fintech company and we do hold a lot of crypto, so it's sort of based on domain knowledge...Most of the returns just comes from domain expertise and sticking within that.
column13: He attributes his edge to operating-business domain expertise and staying within it.

column12: We've only had 3 events like this in 25 years: (2001, 2008–09, 2020)...With a triple rate cut, growth and small caps tend to surge...Institutions are only now adjusting to yesterday’s Fed surprise, rotating into growth assets and the Russell 2000
column13: He builds a macro/institutional-rotation thesis from historical triple-rate-cut analogs to front-run liquidity flows into small caps.

column12: Triple rate cuts were crisis driven previously (2001-.com, 2008-09-financial crisis, 2020-covid). This time Fed cuts before a major crisis = stocks moon immediately.
column13: He distinguishes the current pre-crisis rate-cut regime from prior crisis-driven cuts to argue for immediate equity upside.

column12: I sold all my $TSM calls today $175 for 100%+ profit. I trimmed small caps that were up 20-40%+. Rotating into it into 1 month $NBIS out of money calls and shares
column13: He realizes TSM call gains and rotates trimmed small-cap proceeds into his highest-conviction NBIS.
column16: explicit. Capital rotation into highest conviction after appreciation.

column12: I thought testosterone product launch + PPI would be the main trigger but SI increased from 36 -> 42...A surprise 150% SS would be an acquisition...otherwise looks like one of the gradual ones with short positions covering over time
column13: He revises his squeeze-catalyst expectation, acknowledging shorts added rather than covered and reframing it as a gradual squeeze.
column16: explicit. Catalyst revision when expected trigger fails.

column12: for $HPP, triple rate cut doesn't really affect leasing too much...85.4% of the debt is fixed so it doesn't really benefit from rate cuts...It is below NAV though, usually there's another reason why it's below NAV that other hedge funds priced in.
column13: He rejects an HPP dip-buy by checking fixed-vs-floating debt mix and reasoning that below-NAV usually reflects something already priced.
column16: explicit. Disciplined rejection; respects market pricing of NAV discount.

column12: The most money to be made though is if you think the market prices something wrong and you have conviction otherwise. Like for me market was underpricing $UPWK when it was a p/e of 7
column13: He states his core edge: finding mispricings where he has conviction the market is wrong.

column12: buy stuff at the lows when market gives up on it (eg. Ethereum $1600), and when there's a new narrative with Bitmine, it's a good time to sell at $4k+...This is only different if it's less speculative like NBIS, like a literal $17B contract...Market prices in forward revenue
column13: He lays out a daily framework: buy at capitulation, sell into euphoric narratives, treat contract-backed names differently, and trust forward-revenue pricing.

column12: NEVER USE $HOOD IF YOU HAVE A LARGE PORT...they route your order to MMs...Learn IV of stocks. Eg...NBIS probably doesn't move more than 7% a day. If selling CCs 4 days out...selling 24%+ OTM...is usually smart
column13: He details execution mechanics (PFOF fills) and using per-stock IV and daily-range to set covered-call strikes.
column16: explicit. Options mechanics and IV-based strike selection.

column12: Do not write puts on stocks you're not comfortable buying at those levels...REPEAT with high IV on REALLY good stocks...Do not over-margin extremely high beta stocks, usually 1.3-1.5x...LEARN implied volatility and know WHY it changes.
column13: He gives a detailed cash-secured-put framework keyed to IV bands, beta-adjusted margin, bottom-timing, and only writing on names he'd own.

column12: ETOR...$700M+ cash pile on a 3.3B market cap, compounding similar rate to IBKR...PLTR, cult stock detached from fundamentals, large part of their profit is just interest income
column13: His rated buy/sell list pairs each name with a fundamental or catalyst reason, separating quality businesses from price-detached cult stocks.
column16: explicit. Reasoned ratings; good company vs good stock distinction. VIRT quantified DD in quoted text (forward P/E, floating-rate interest savings, buybacks).

column12: The best advice you will ever get is free DD on X, or posts coming from randoms dropping $NBIS / $IREN. Not 🤡 Wave 69 XYZ $BMNR charting for $20.
column13: He dismisses paywalled wave-count TA, contrasting it with fundamental/catalyst DD as the real source of edge.
column16: explicit. Methodology stance: fundamentals over chart patterns.

column12: $BMNR is an $ETH holding company. Ignoring $ETH and trying to chart $BMNR makes anyone laugh. Fundamentally $BMNR tracks $ETH and it’s roughly 1.3x NAV. For $BMNR to go up 100%, ETH would need to go up 82%.
column13: He decomposes a crypto-treasury stock to its underlying-asset/NAV relationship to show charting it is meaningless.
column16: explicit. Translates structure into a NAV-linked valuation.

column12: I think $ETOR might have been my only cost-avg loss so far this year. It important to post unrealized losses as well. I don't think I took into Cathie Wood buying up the stock...into my calculations since stocks usually do the reverse when she enters/exits.
column13: He admits a losing position and identifies a missed variable (ARK ownership as a contrarian signal) in his analysis.
column16: explicit. Self-critique and variable he overlooked.

column12: I broke my own rule of thumb here about shares only vs call -> 32% IV stock but usually if something black swans off something immaterial eg $IREN 14% drop off JPM bs price target...then I switch to calls.
column13: He states his rule of using shares by default but switching to calls when low-IV names get hit by immaterial news at a likely bottom.

column12: I typically cost average down for swing trading. Some levels I look out for...(i don't leverage unless something is extremely oversold)...$SG (High $13) buy, (~$12.8) very strong buy, sub $11 calls.
column13: He describes a laddered cost-averaging entry framework with leverage reserved for extreme oversold levels.

column12: Mag7 is AI compute strained, by design from $NVDA. Trillions of capex that normally flowed through AWS, MSFT Azure, Google Cloud for traditional compute, will now funnel into NeoClouds when they cant handle new AI loads
column13: He starts from the physical reality that hyperscalers are compute-constrained and traces where capex must flow before picking neocloud securities.

column12: NVDA / TSM (2022->): GPU for hyperscalers. CRDO / ALAB (2024 ->): hyperscaler wins -> parabolic growth. NBIS/CIFR/IREN/etc (2025 - ) AWS/Azure/etc. -> parabolic growth from AI compute
column13: He maps successive supply-chain layers and which layer captured parabolic earnings growth in each wave to identify where value rotates next.

column12: You have 4B+ in funding for $NBIS at $138+ a share (when it's $107 now)... ABOVE current price funding is a bullish tell.
column13: He uses the strike price of fundraising rounds above the current share price as a measurable bullish signal of insider conviction.

column12: Custom hyperscaler chips like TPU, Trainium. But likely years away, since they're still begging for NVDA compute and signed 5Y-10Y contracts.
column13: He explicitly enumerates substitute risks (custom chips, NVDA GPU-as-a-service, GPU depreciation) and times them against locked-in long-term contracts.

column12: Traders consider sector momentum, catalysts, valuation, pullbacks, macro, IV, option flows, etc.
column13: He lists the full set of measurable factors he weighs per name rather than a single 'be patient' heuristic.

column12: $HIMS... this was actually caused from something material, which was Trump launching a direct to consumer GOV drug website... I still remember AMZN launching a competitor, HIMS crashed 20% then rose again
column13: He distinguishes a material catalyst from a non-material dip, using a prior analog to judge whether the news is a real headwind or a buy.

column12: portfolio positioning is extremely important just in case you get timing wrong... $NBIS drops $117-> $112, you can do like $15k at that level. If it drops $112 -> $105, do another $15k. If it drop $105 -> $95, you can switch that $30k to calls.
column13: He describes a laddered DCA position-sizing rule that reserves cash so wrong timing can be averaged down and only escalates to calls at deep dips.

column12: I didn't have enough time to do a deep dive into the META contract yet... I'll probably spend an hour or two to look into the contract + valuation after I eat some salad... maybe the meta contracts would make me reconsider this as a long
column13: After a fast 13% trade he commits to re-underwriting CRWV on the new Meta contract before deciding whether to hold it as a long.

column12: History doesn't repeat itself but it often rhymes for companies in similar sectors. With things material to fundamentals: Eg. $NBIS (64->100->86) vs. $CIFR... We'll likely see CIFR increase like NBIS after the dip.
column13: He uses sector-peer pattern analogs (rise-dip-ascent post hyperscaler deal) as evidence, but only when a fundamental change drives the move.

column12: Do not write puts on stocks you're not comfortable buying at those levels... please do not write options on random penny stocks... LEARN implied volatility and know WHY it changes... Also need to know any major macro events
column13: He lays out a put-selling risk framework: only sell puts on names you'd own, understand IV drivers, avoid earnings weeks, and size margin to beta.

column12: $RGTI is an amazing short. But you do realize that the reason the IONQ/RGTI marketcap is $13B+ is largely driven by short interest.
column13: He separates a fundamentally weak company from an un-shortable stock because short-interest dynamics inflate the market cap of cult names.

column12: Short interest at 15% is still considered slightly elevated. On $RGTI it was 25.1% earlier this year, now it's 14.4%, buying back 10% of the float on the ramp up causes this elevated MC... This is why I don't short cult stocks
column13: He explains why he refuses to short cult stocks: short covering on declining SI mechanically lifts market cap.

column12: $SNAP net income will probably shoot up a ton after deleting everyone's memories or making people pay. The amount of money they spend on Google Cloud is absolutely absurd. Their revenue numbers are great, net profit not so much.
column13: He translates Snap's engineering cost structure (cloud storage of Memories) into the reason for low net profit and the lever for re-rating.

column12: The people who sell $10 courses on X just pick anything like $RGTI that went up 4000% off 0 revenue... When I do stock lists, I write down transparently: A light explanation of catalysts or fundamental changes
column13: He contrasts hype tickers with zero revenue against his own practice of attaching a catalyst or fundamental change to every call.

column12: Fintech, Crypto, AI. I have some patents/papers in crypto, semiconductors, + AI space... Stuff outside like biotech, construction, real estate, agriculture, etc. I wouldn't know well.
column13: He explicitly defines his circle of competence (fintech/crypto/AI/semis from patents) and the domains he won't give directional opinions on.

column12: Snap brings in $1.35B revenue a quarter... Despite large revenue numbers, they're not profitable. Why? Because countless people... have stored GBs of videos... Snapchat has been paying Google Cloud to store all of that for free.
column13: In his SNAP thesis he builds a full OPEX-to-FCF model: cloud cost baseline, storage deletion savings, paid-storage revenue, then an EV/FCF re-rating.
column16: explicit. Full Snapchat thesis with numerical buildup.

column12: Only downside is Snap's memory monetization is 1 year out materially. Markets are usually forward looking, but may wait for more confirmation before fully pricing it in
column13: He flags the market-awareness gap: the catalyst is real but a year out, so he expects delayed pricing-in even from forward-looking markets.

column12: this is outside my domain, which is why I wanted to see if anyone else with any domain knowledge had feedback or red flags. (disclosure I bought $50k worth just as a high risk gamble)
column13: On a biotech name outside his domain he sizes it as a small gamble and crowdsources domain experts to find red flags rather than trusting authority.

column12: SPRB has $16.4M in cash equivalents, June 30, 2025, dilution with new shares seems inevitable... After issuing new shares + dilution... don't really see the $500 figure if dilution is included.
column13: He re-runs the SPRB target after reading the balance sheet, concluding dilution risk caps fair value far below the bull's $500 PT.
column16: explicit. Thesis revision/caution from balance-sheet read.

column12: Oracle faced... Low utilization (idle capacity) before customer ramp... Result: Negative gross margins on GPU compute... Neoclouds like $NBIS do it better and this is an actual competitive moat
column13: He reads Oracle's negative GPU-compute margins as engineering proof of complexity that validates specialized neoclouds' moat and pricing power.

column12: Part of $ORCL's loss was time between buildout/utilization since virtually all of its deals came from 4-5 customers... $NBIS for example is heavily diversified and would likely achieve the high utilization throughout buildout.
column13: He identifies customer concentration plus buildout-to-utilization lag as the chokepoint differentiating winners; diversified clients sustain utilization.

column12: Institutional ownership is low on the infra of mag7 for AI compute so they probably use these opportunities to accumulate from retail.
column13: He uses low institutional ownership to argue funds accumulate from panicking retail on dips, an institutional-rotation timing read.

column12: $WYFI's edge is probably converting facilities for 40% lower cost than greenfield data centers... they own the infrastructure instead of leasing... Downside is they don't build their own orchestration stack like $NBIS or scale as fast as $IREN.
column13: He compares neocloud peers layer by layer (capex cost basis, ownership vs lease, orchestration software, scaling speed) to rank value capture.

column12: I trusted the news + other posts about institutions selling. Did more research and it turns out to be a 445m share unlock, which was 30% of the float... it's a seriously material event.
column13: He publicly retracts and deletes his BULL dip-buy call after discovering the drop was a 30%-of-float unlock, not a one-off block sale.

column12: $MU - Memory in demand given the amount of infra required by Stargate + OpenAI
column13: He adds memory (Micron) by reasoning from the physical memory intensity of Stargate/OpenAI buildout.

column12: I go through tons of companies every day. I was looking at $DFLI... Typed in fly, found $FLY. Looked at it, and they have contracts with Northrop Grumman, military contracts, and were building medium-lift reusable rockets.
column13: He describes his discovery funnel: screening many small caps daily by market cap, then digging into ones with contracts and a moat.

column12: Northrop's prior launcher, Antares 230 worked before Russia's 2022 invasion... then the supply chain was cut off... it couldn't build its own first stage anymore. So they went looking for a U.S. partner with ready-made propulsion: Firefly.
column13: His FLY thesis reverse-maps the rocket supply chain: severed Russian first-stage supply forces Northrop to source Firefly's Miranda engines as a plug-and-play replacement.

column12: Northrop's system + Firefly's new-space engines hits a scarce, high-demand moat (U.S. medium-lift with reuse), already validated by TacRS and a $1B+ backlog + US golden dome defense system catalyst.
column13: He frames U.S. reusable medium-lift as a scarce indispensable capability (only SpaceX exists) with a backlog and defense catalyst, an asymmetric small-cap bet vs the giant partner.

column12: the market is already pricing in the feature of failure. The bears are pointing to Alpha's failures... HOWEVER, they're pricing the risk in completely wrong... The question they should be asking is, whether both Northrop + Firefly can make this work?
column13: He identifies the mispricing: bears extrapolate Firefly's solo Alpha failures, missing that Northrop co-development changes the risk equation.

column12: What this typically foreshadows is slower growth and higher inflation... many stocks dropped in crossfire without any direct exposure, such as $UNH or $DKNG... fell 9.4%... unlike $QLCM, $NKE, $MU, $CDNS
column13: On the tariff shock he separates names with real China exposure from those that dropped in crossfire, identifying second-order/no-exposure dislocations to buy.

column12: I might think $AMD calls should print because they're fabless but obviously near term it's a headwind to the semi industry with rare earth export controls.
column13: He uses AMD's fabless structure plus rare-earth export controls to judge near-term semi headwinds versus longer-term upside.

column12: Nebius is full stack -> higher margin (software orchestration for GPU utilization... and why $CRWV bought out a lot of software companies)... sum of parts... more diversified client base (learned from $ORCL why that's important)
column13: He ranks NBIS above peers using three layer-comparison criteria he derived from the ORCL and CRWV evidence: full-stack margin, sum-of-parts, client diversification.

column12: $RKLB -> I knew Space was rapidly growing since Trump announced Space force... Around the $28 mark... they started developing a medium-lift payload, SpaceX raised a large round at $350B, and I started seeing the potential
column13: He narrates exactly when and why conviction crystallized for each long (the trigger event that converted a watch into high conviction).

column12: OpEx spend: 60-75% of revenue goes to marketing. This is a huge positive... long term when they cut back on spend, customers are sticky and this helps a lot with profitability... unlike Snapchat google cloud opex.
column13: In his RBRK DD he reads the OPEX composition (marketing vs cloud) as the key to future FCF, contrasting discretionary spend with structural cost.

column12: critical minerals are really complex. When I buy stuff like $MP I usually use ChatGPT deep research to help me understand risk-reward... when I buy random lithium battery small caps... I admit I don't have domain knowledge.
column13: He admits using AI research tools to compensate outside his domain and caps portfolio weight on names where he lacks expertise.
